retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab is a monoclonal antibody that selectively binds to the interleukin-6 receptor and prevents IL-6 from binding to its receptor (IL-6R) on the liver, lung, and synovial fibroblasts, as examples, to treat autoimmune and inflammatory conditions. [7] IL-6 is an important mediator of chronic inflammation. In the liver, IL-6 can increase the production of C-reactive protein (CRP), serum amyloid A, fibrinogen, and hepcidin and decrease albumin synthesis. These liver mediator effects can cause amyloidosis, cardiovascular events, edema, and anemia. Tocilizumab's IL-6R blocking effects in the lungs are the mechanism for treating COVID-19 pneumonia. IL-6 can activate synovial fibroblasts in the joints to increase angiogenesis, vascular permeability, and the production of vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). This can lead to increased edema and joint swelling, most commonly in the hands. Thus, tocilizumab's blocking of IL-6 effects in synovial fibroblasts would be beneficial in treating rheumatoid arthritis. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibodies: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a proinflammatory cytokine considered the key driver of the hyperinflammatory state associated with COVID-19. Targeting this cytokine with an IL-6 receptor inhibitor could slow down the process of inflammation based on case reports that showed favorable outcomes in patients with severe COVID-19. [108] [109] [110]  The FDA approved three different types of IL-6 receptor inhibitors for various rheumatological conditions (Tocilizumab, Sarilumab) and a rare disorder called Castleman's syndrome (siltuximab). Tocilizumab is an anti-interleukin-6 receptor alpha receptor monoclonal antibody indicated for various rheumatological diseases. The data regarding the use of this agent is mixed. A randomized control trial involving 438 hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 pneumonia, among which 294 were randomized to receive tocilizumab and 144 to placebo, showed that tocilizumab did not translate into a significant improvement in clinical status or lower the 28-day mortality compared to placebo. [111] Results from another randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial involving patients with confirmed severe COVID-19 that involved 243 patients randomized to receive tocilizumab or placebo showed that the use of tocilizumab was ineffective in preventing intubation or death rate. [112] The REMAP-CAP and RECOVERY trials (not yet published), two large randomized controlled trials, showed evidence of a mortality benefit in patients exhibiting rapid respiratory decompensation. [113] Sarilumab and Siltuximab are IL-6 receptor antagonists that may potentially have a similar effect on the hyperinflammatory state associated with COVID-19 as tocilizumab. Currently, no known published clinical trials support the use of siltuximab in severe COVID-19. Conversely, a 60-day randomized, double-blind placebo control multinational phase 3 trial that evaluated the clinical efficacy, mortality, and safety of sarilumab in 431 patients showed no significant improvement in clinical status or mortality rate. [114] Another randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study on sarilumab's clinical efficacy and safety in adult patients hospitalized with COVID-19 is ongoing (NCT04315298 ).

retrieved_docs.title: Type III Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management -- Rheumatoid Arthritis
retrieved_docs.content: The treatment of rheumatoid arthritis centers around controlling synovitis, preventing joint damage, relieving pain, and preserving musculoskeletal function. Regular physical activity is imperative, and patients should receive proper instruction from a physical therapist and occupational therapist about specific movements that will increase strength and mobility without worsening joint inflammation. Unless contraindicated, all patients with rheumatoid arthritis should receive the disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) methotrexate  as well as NSAIDs or glucocorticoids as needed for symptomatic relief. Patients who fail methotrexate alone may try triple therapy with methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, and sulfasalazine or add a tumor necrosis factor (TNF) such as etanercept to methotrexate. If a TNF is inappropriate, then methotrexate plus abatacept, a cytotoxic T-lymphocyte antigen-4, tocilizumab, a humanized IgG1 anti-human IL-6 receptor antibody, leflunomide, or sarilumab, a human monoclonal antibody directed against the membrane-bound and soluble IL-6R, are acceptable alternatives. [43] Methotrexate plus rituximab or a Janus kinase inhibitor, such as tofacitinib, are less commonly used combinations.

retrieved_docs.title: Bispecific Antibody Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Immunosuppression
retrieved_docs.content: In grades 3 to 4 CRS, dexamethasone 8 mg every 8 hours intravenously or orally should be administered for 3 days. Steroids should be tapered over 4 days afterward. An inadequate response to these measures warrants intravenous tocilizumab at a dose of 8 mg/kg. Tocilizumab is an IL-6 antagonist that has produced good responses in severe CRS.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Pediatric patients
retrieved_docs.content: See dosing for juvenile patients above.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Mechanism of Action -- Elimination
retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab's half-life is 5 to 13 days. Clearance appears to be dose-dependent, non-linear at lower doses, and linear at higher doses.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Adult Dosing, Dosage Forms and Strength
retrieved_docs.content: COVID-19 8 mg/kg/dose IV for a single dose.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify patients suitable for tocilizumab therapy based on their clinical presentation and disease characteristics. Implement appropriate protocols for administering tocilizumab, including dosage adjustments and tailored treatment strategies. Assess patient response to tocilizumab therapy, monitoring therapeutic efficacy and potential adverse effects. Implement effective communication with patients about tocilizumab therapy's benefits, risks, and potential outcomes, ensuring informed decision-making. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Adult Dosing, Dosage Forms and Strength
retrieved_docs.content: Cytokine release syndrome >30 kg: 8 mg/kg IV, maximum 800 mg/dose; may be used as monotherapy or in combination with a glucocorticoid. If no clinical improvement occurs after the first dose, 3 additional doses may be administered in at least 8-hour intervals.

retrieved_docs.title: Rheumatoid Arthritis -- Treatment / Management -- Interleukin 6 Inhibitors
retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab, an IL-6 receptor inhibitor, is indicated for moderate-to-severe active RA in adults who have had an inadequate response to TNF inhibitor therapy. These patients develop clinically meaningful improvement with the use of tocilizumab. [110] Sarilumab is another agent in this category that has been shown to improve clinical outcomes in patients with uncontrolled RA despite TNF inhibitor therapy. [111]

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Adult Dosing, Dosage Forms and Strength
retrieved_docs.content: Tocilizumab is administered intravenously (IV) or subcutaneously (SQ).

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The healthcare team should monitor the patient for signs or symptoms of infection, eg, tuberculosis, candidiasis, etc. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Transplantation Rejection -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-IL-6 receptor antibodies: Tocilizumab inhibits IL-6 signaling, attenuating inflammatory responses and graft damage. These antibodies show promise in preventing acute rejection in liver transplants.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Pregnant women
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should weigh the risk vs benefit in pregnant patients. Insufficient human data is available, but there is a possible risk of embryo-fetal toxicity based on animal studies.

retrieved_docs.content: (zanamivir), fusion inhibitor (enfuvirtide), CCR5‑targeting agents (maraviroc, ibalizumab), CMV kinase inhibitor (maribavir), broad‑spectrum agents (moroxydine, brivudine, brincidofovir, remdesivir, tecovirimat, tilorone, fostemsavir), and monoclonal antibodies (tanezumab, abciximab, ibalizumab). Notable drug‑drug interactions include the contraindication of pantoprazole with the NNRTI rilpivirine and the metabolic contraindication of gemfibrozil with the insulin‑secretagogue repaglinide. Methotrexate sodium is contraindicated in patients with HIV infection, and gold‑based antirheumatic agents (aurothioglucose, aurothiomalate sodium) are placed in the allergen structural class because of allergic potential. The anti‑inflammatory and antirheumatic ATC branch separates non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) from disease‑modifying antirheumatic drugs. NSAID families include acetic‑acid derivatives (sulindak group), fenamates (mefenamic acid, tofenamic acid), propionic‑acid

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: group and is used to treat viral infection; ribavirin, another nucleoside analogue, is noted for preventing viral infection. The ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use” comprises the lipopeptide antifungals kaspofungin and micafungin. “Other antivirals in ATC” include bulevirtide (a lipopeptidy compound), enfuvirtide, maraviroc, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, tecovirimat, and tiloron. Bulevirtide’s lipopeptidy structure links it to other lipopeptide agents. Ibalizumab and abciximab are monoclonal antibodies, the latter primarily serving as an antiplatelet agent. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat exhibit activity against smallpox (الجدري), a viral infection. These shared scaffolds and ATC classifications illustrate how chemical structure, mechanism of action, and therapeutic indication intersect, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate antiviral, antifungal, or antibody‑based therapies while recognizing important contraindications and epidemiologic risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: are mapped from an oligopeptidyl scaffold, indicating a common biochemical origin. Additional antiviral agents include nucleosides/nucleotides that are not RT inhibitors (brincidofovir, the bromodeoxyuridine analogue brivudine, remdesivir), the lipopeptide bulevirtide, and ATC “Other antivirals” such as maribavir, moroxydine, tecovirimat and tilorone. Both ibalizumab and abciximab are monoclonal antibodies that target specific proteins; tanezumab is also represented as a monoclonal antibody (inverse‑ISA relationship). The antifungal ATC “Other antimycotics for systemic use” contains the lipopeptidic echinocandins kaspofungin and micafungin. Remdesivir is recognized for treatment of viral infections, ribavirin may be employed for prophylaxis, and smallpox (an orthopoxvirus infection) can be managed with brincidofovir and tecovirimat. Collectively, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure in which systemic antivirals contain DAAs organized by chemical class, mechanism and

Monoclonal antibody therapeutics include abciximab and ibalizumab, the latter also belonging to the “other antivirals” ATC category. In the antifungal domain, systemic antimycotics comprise the lipopeptide caspofungin and micafungin. Collectively, these relationships delineate the pharmacologic classifications, chemical families, therapeutic indications, contraindications, and formulation hierarchies of a diverse set of agents used in gastrointestinal, antiviral, antiretroviral, antifungal, and anticoagulant therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 0 -- Community 9
retrieved_docs.content: ibalizumab, bulevirtide, fostemsavir; • Other agents – moroxydine, tiloron, tecovirimat, brincidofovir, brivudine, remdesivir. NRTIs act as nucleoside analogues that terminate HIV reverse transcription, whereas non‑RTI nucleoside‑like agents (brincidofovir, brivudine) inhibit viral DNA polymerases and remdesivir targets the RNA‑dependent RNA polymerase of SARS‑CoV‑2. Bulevirtide, enfuvirtide, fostemsavir and ibalizumab block viral entry; tecovirimat and tiloron impede viral egress; moroxydine interferes with viral DNA replication. Lipopeptide chemistry links bulevirtide and the echinocandin antifungal caspofungin, the latter together with micafungin inhibiting β‑(1,3)‑D‑glucan synthesis in fungal cell walls. Monoclonal antibodies ibalizumab and abciximab expand therapeutic options beyond small molecules. Adjunctive agents include the antibiotic aztreonam (1000 mg injectable) and the cholesterol‑binding resin cholestyramine (4000 mg oral suspension). Pre‑exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other antivirals in ATC” encompasses a diverse set of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtid, maribavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron, all of which are classified within this ATC subgroup. In addition to its antiviral activity, ibalizumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, linking it to the biologic class “monoklonální protilátky.” Similarly, abciximab is explicitly described as a monoclonal antibody. This overlap illustrates that some agents within the “Other antivirals in ATC” group, such as ibalizumab, serve dual roles as both antiviral drugs and monoclonal antibody therapeutics, highlighting the convergence of antiviral pharmacotherapy and targeted biologic treatments in modern patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies (monoklonální protilátky) comprise a therapeutic class that includes abciximab, ibalizumab and tanezumab. abciximab is explicitly classified as a monoclonal antibody, and ibalizumab is both mapped to and recognized as a monoclonal antibody; the inverse‑isa relationship also indicates that tanezumab belongs to the monoclonal‑antibody group. In addition to their immunologic mechanisms, certain monoclonal antibodies function as antiviral agents and are therefore grouped within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category “Other antivirals in ATC.” This antiviral class contains enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, tiloron, and ibalizumab. The inclusion of ibalizumab in both categories reflects its dual role as a monoclonal antibody and as an antiviral medication (e.g., an entry inhibitor for HIV). Together, these relationships illustrate how specific biologic agents can be classified by both their molecular nature (monoclonal antibodies) and their therapeutic application (antiviral drugs), which is important for accurate drug categorization, prescribing, and patient management.

In the respiratory domain, gemifloxacin treats a form of pneumonia that can arise as a complication of influenza, and zanamivir may be used to prevent influenza. A clinically important drug interaction is the contraindication of the proton‑pump inhibitor pantoprazole with the NNRTI rilpivirine. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how antiviral drugs, nucleoside analogues, monoclonal antibodies, lipopeptides and other direct‑acting

Therapeutic regimens employ NRTIs (tenofovir disoproxil, abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine), integrase inhibitors (cabotegravir, dolutegravir, raltegravir), NNRTIs (doravirine, delavirdine, efavirenz, etravirine, nevirapine, rilpivirine), the CCR5 antagonist maraviroc, and the attachment inhibitor fostemsavir. Doravirine is contraindicated when co‑administered with the rifamycin rifapentine. Additional antiviral agents are classified by scaffold: lipopeptidyl‑derived compounds include bulevirtide and caspofungin (the latter also listed among systemic antimycotics with micafungin), while monoclonal antibodies such as abciximab and ibalizumab target specific immune pathways. Bulevirtide, enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab and tecovirimat belong to the ATC “Other antivirals” group; bulevirtide shares the lipopeptidyl scaffold. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 0 -- Community 18
retrieved_docs.content: related nucleoside analogues include abacavir and its fixed‑dose combinations (related to dideoxyadenosine) and brivudine (a bromodeoxyuridine analogue). Monoclonal antibodies in this group are abciximab and ibalizumab. Remdesivir is indicated for treatment of viral infections, while ribavirin may be employed for viral infection prophylaxis. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are used in the treatment of smallpox. Zanamivir is employed for influenza prophylaxis; influenza, an acute respiratory viral infection, can present as pneumonia with nodular infiltrates, a condition also treatable with the antibacterial gemifloxacin. In the antifungal domain, the ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use” includes the echinocandin agents caspofungin (a lipopeptide) and micafungin. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 0 -- Community 8
retrieved_docs.content: gp120 attachment inhibitors (fostemsavir), post‑attachment monoclonal antibodies (ibalizumab), and other antivirals listed in the ATC system. Ophthalmic formulations such as famciclovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridin, and trifluridin are classified under the Czech term “antivirové látky,” denoting antiviral substances rather than prostaglandins. Interferon alfa‑2a represents an antiviral cytokine, while pentamidine is a parent compound within the antiviral hierarchy. Nucleic‑acid‑synthesis inhibitors that are not reverse‑transcriptase inhibitors include ganciklovir, cidofovir, brincidofovir, brivudine, and remdesivir; brincidofovir and tecovirimat are indicated for smallpox, and remdesivir has broad antiviral applicability. Additional “Other antivirals” comprise bulevirtide (lipopeptide), enfuvirtide, maribavir, moroxydine, ribavirin (preventive use), and zanamivir (influenza prophylaxis). Monoclonal antibodies appear both as antiviral biologics (tanezumab, ibalizumab) and as non‑antiviral

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 486
retrieved_docs.content: Crizanlizumab is categorized within the therapeutic class Immunological Agents [TC] and is described as an Immunomodulator (disposition), indicating that it functions as an immunomodulatory agent. It is identified as a Selective adhesion molecule inhibitor (product), reflecting its mechanism of targeting adhesion molecules. In addition, crizanlizumab is characterized as both imunomodulátory and imunosupresiva, underscoring its dual role as an immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive medication. This collection of classifications highlights crizanlizumab’s role in modulating immune responses through selective adhesion molecule inhibition, which is pertinent to therapeutic strategies for inflammatory and vaso‑occlusive disorders.

Ophthalmologic antivirals listed are famciclovir, ganciclovir, idoxuridine and trifluridine; ganciclovir functions as a nucleic‑acid synthesis inhibitor and idoxuridine, a metabolic descendant of deoxyuridine, may prevent viral infection. HIV infection is identified epidemiologically with shared needle use. A broad spectrum of antiretroviral agents is employed for both pre‑exposure prophylaxis (dolutegravir, efavirenz, emtricitabine, etravirine, ibalizumab, lamivudine, nevirapine, raltegravir, rilpivirine, stavudine, zidovudine) and treatment of established infection (cabotegravir, fostemsavir, maraviroc). Important drug‑class interactions are highlighted: doravirine is contraindicated with rifapentine, and pantoprazole is contraindicated with rilpivirine. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: The group labeled “Other antivirals in ATC” encompasses a diverse set of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtid, maravirok, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron. Within this collection, ibalizumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, linking it to the broader class of monoklonální protilátky (monoclonal antibodies). Similarly, abciximab is classified as a monoclonal antibody. This illustrates that the “Other antivirals in ATC” category contains both small‑molecule entry inhibitors such as enfuvirtid, maravirok, and fostemsavir, as well as biologic therapies like ibalizumab and abciximab, highlighting the varied mechanisms and formats of antiviral treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 103
retrieved_docs.content: Monoclonal antibodies (monoklonální protilátky) constitute a therapeutic class that includes tanezumab, abciximab, and ibalizumab. Tanezumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, abciximab is classified as a monoclonal antibody, and ibalizumab is mapped to the monoclonal antibody class. Within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system, the category “Other antivirals in ATC” comprises the antiviral agents enfuvirtid, maravirok, moroxydine, fostemsavir, and ibalizumab. Consequently, ibalizumab uniquely belongs to both the monoclonal antibody group and the “Other antivirals in ATC” group, highlighting an overlap between biologic immunotherapies and antiviral treatments. Recognizing these interconnections assists clinicians in understanding the dual therapeutic roles of certain agents and in navigating classification schemes for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 1 -- Community 132
retrieved_docs.content: Carglumic acid, crizanlizumab, and dextrose monohydrate are all agents that affect blood or body fluids. Crizanlizumab is further defined as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor and is placed within the therapeutic class of immunological agents. In clinical pharmacology it is regarded as an immunomodulator and also exhibits immunosuppressive activity, reflecting its capacity to alter immune pathways. Carglumic acid shares the broader classification of an agent affecting blood or body fluids, while dextrose monohydrate is likewise categorized in this group. Recognizing these interrelated classifications helps clinicians understand the mechanisms of action and therapeutic contexts of each agent, guiding appropriate patient care decisions.
